---
title: "Web Architecture and REST"
lang: en-GB
---

## From Message to Resource — Evolution of Communication Models in Healthcare {#sec-rest-evolucao}

### The message model and its limitations

`HL7` v2 solved a fundamental problem: it eliminated the manual exchange
of data between hospital information systems and created an automatic
model based on clinical messages. However, as hospital networks grew
and inter-institutional interoperability became necessary, the structural
limits of the model became evident [@BensonGrieve2021].

The central problem is **tight coupling**: in a message-based model, each
pair of systems must be configured individually. If a hospital has N
systems (HIS, LIS, RIS, PACS, pharmacy, etc.), the number of interfaces
to configure and maintain grows on the order of N², since each system
must have a specific interface for every other. With ten systems, there
are potentially ninety bilateral interfaces. Each new interface requires
analysis, development, testing and ongoing maintenance. Scalability is
structurally limited.

Alongside this, the message model is *push-based*: information is
transmitted when an event occurs, not when it is needed. An external
system that needs to query the current state of a patient cannot simply
ask — it must wait for the relevant event to generate a message and
deliver it. Ad hoc querying is not part of the model.

The following table compares the two models across the dimensions
relevant to modern interoperability:

| Dimension | Message model (HL7 v2) | Resource model (`REST`) |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Initiative | *Push* — the sending system decides when to transmit | *Pull* — the client decides when to query |
| Coupling | Tight — A knows B and vice versa | Loose — the client only knows the `URL` |
| Scale | N² interfaces | Any client, any server |
| Prior configuration | Required per pair of systems | Not required |
| Formats | Positional text (pipes and carets) | `JSON` over `HTTP` |
| Ad hoc querying | Not provided | Native |

: Comparison between the message model (HL7 v2) and the resource model (REST) [@BensonGrieve2021; @Fielding2000]. {#tbl-modelos-comparacao}

### From `SOAP`/`XML` to `REST`/`JSON`

Before `REST` established itself as the dominant paradigm, integration
between more recent healthcare systems relied on **Web Services**
`SOAP` — Simple Object Access Protocol — a communication protocol based
on `XML` that structures messages in a rigid format called an *envelope*:
a structure with header and body that wraps the data to be transmitted.
HL7 v3, released in 2005, was designed precisely to use this model:
voluminous `XML` messages transported in `SOAP` *envelopes*, consumed
by Web Services servers, managed by specialised *middleware*
[@BensonGrieve2021]. Middleware — intermediary software that mediates
communication between systems — was mandatory in HL7 v3 architectures.
In practice, it required specialised integration engines such as Mirth
Connect or Rhapsody, responsible for receiving, transforming and routing
`SOAP` messages.

Complexity was its greatest obstacle. A typical `SOAP` request required:
generating the `XML` *envelope*, configuring the Web Services server,
installing and maintaining integration middleware (of the
`ESB` — Enterprise Service Bus type), and dealing with a data model
(`RIM` — Reference Information Model) too abstract for direct
implementation. In many institutions, this implementation cost seriously
limited the adoption of HL7 v3 [@BensonGrieve2021].

`REST` proposes a radically simpler model: any HTTP server can serve
clinical resources. It need not be a specific server (that is, a
dedicated special webservice). A request is purely HTTP — a
`GET /Patient/123` — and the response is `JSON`. There is no `SOAP`
*envelope*, no mandatory middleware. Any web developer can implement a
`REST` server without knowledge of hospital middleware — the barrier to
entry dropped drastically.

### The web as universal infrastructure

`HTTP` — HyperText Transfer Protocol — has existed since 1991. It is
the most widely deployed application protocol in the world: supported
by every programming language, operating system and device [@RFC9110].
Its adoption as the transport for clinical interoperability was not
accidental — it represents the deliberate choice of a universally
available infrastructure over a proprietary stack.

This choice has direct practical consequences:

- Any hospital with a web server can expose clinical data via `REST`
- The development and debugging tools are the same used in any web
  project
- Security (`HTTPS`, token-based authentication) is shared with the
  global web ecosystem
- Maintenance draws on a vast knowledge base of web developers

It is this alignment with the universal infrastructure of the web that
explains why FHIR, the modern clinical interoperability standard, adopted
`REST` as its native communication model.

## The `REST` Model — Principles, *Constraints* and *Stateless* {#sec-rest-modelo}

### What `REST` is

`REST` — Representational State Transfer — is an architectural style for
distributed systems, proposed by Roy Fielding in his doctoral dissertation
in 2000 [@Fielding2000]. It is not a protocol, not a formal standard,
not a data format. It is a set of constraints that, when respected,
produce systems with specific properties: scalability, simplicity and
interoperability.

The distinction matters: a system can use `HTTP` and `JSON` without being
`REST`; and it can be `REST` without using `JSON`. What qualifies a system
as *RESTful* (conformant with `REST`) is adherence to the architectural
constraints defined by Fielding [@Fielding2000].

### The six architectural constraints

Fielding defined six constraints. A system that satisfies all of them is
said to be *RESTful*:

**1. Client–server** — Separation of concerns: the client manages the
interface and user experience; the server manages data and business
logic. The two evolve independently. A FHIR server can change its
internal implementation without requiring clients to be updated —
provided the `REST` interface remains stable.

**2. *Stateless*** — The server retains no session state between
requests. Each client request contains all the information necessary to
be processed independently: resource identity, authentication credentials,
search parameters. This constraint is the most critical for scalability —
see the following section.

**3. *Cacheable*** — Responses must indicate whether they may be stored
in cache. Resources that change infrequently (such as terminologies or
lists of specialties) can be cached by the client, reducing server load
and response latency.

**4. Uniform interface** — The interface between client and server is
standardised and predictable, regardless of the resource type. It rests
on four sub-constraints: identification of resources by `URI`
— Uniform Resource Identifier; manipulation of resources through their
representations; self-descriptive messages (each message contains
sufficient information to be interpreted without external context); and
`HATEOAS` — Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State — the server
includes in its responses links to the next possible states.

**5. Layered system** — The client need not know whether it communicates
directly with the final server or with intermediaries (proxies, load
balancers, API gateways). Layers are transparent to the client. This
property enables horizontal scaling and the addition of security without
altering the interface.

**6. Code on demand** *(optional)* — The server may send executable code
to the client (for example, JavaScript). It is the only optional
constraint. In clinical contexts, this capability is rarely used; most
`REST` implementations in healthcare ignore this constraint without
ceasing to be considered *RESTful*.

### *Stateless* — the central principle

Of all the constraints, *stateless* is the one with the greatest
practical impact. Understanding it in depth is essential to appreciating
why `REST` scales where other models fail.

In a *stateful* model, the server maintains a session per client: it
knows who is connected, what was previously requested, language
preferences, and the current point in navigation. The client need only
send the differences relative to the previous state. This reduces the
volume of data transmitted — but creates a rigid dependency between client
and server. If the server fails, the session is lost. Horizontal scaling
(adding servers) requires session synchronisation across instances
[@Fielding2000].

In a *stateless* model, each request is completely autonomous. The client
always sends all required information: the resource identity, credentials,
parameters. The server processes and responds with no memory of previous
requests. If the server fails, the client can resend exactly the same
request to a different server with no loss of context. Any server
instance can respond to any request — horizontal scalability is immediate
[@Fielding2000; @RichardsonRuby2007].

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: stateful vs stateless.
  Two panels side by side. Teal palette.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="240" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- Título -->
  <text x="340" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Stateful vs Stateless</text>

  <!-- STATEFUL (esq) -->
  <rect x="20" y="35" width="300" height="185" rx="8"
        fill="#0a3d4a" fill-opacity="0.06" stroke="#0a3d4a" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="170" y="55" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Stateful</text>

  <!-- Cliente A -->
  <rect x="35" y="65" width="80" height="30" rx="5" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="75" y="85" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Client A</text>

  <!-- Arrow → server with session -->
  <line x1="117" y1="80" x2="178" y2="80" stroke="#0d7c8c"
        stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#sf1)"/>
  <text x="148" y="73" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"
        fill="#0d7c8c">request 2</text>

  <!-- Servidor stateful -->
  <rect x="180" y="60" width="120" height="130" rx="6" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="240" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Server</text>
  <rect x="192" y="88" width="96" height="18" rx="3" fill="#fff" fill-opacity="0.15"/>
  <text x="240" y="101" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"
        fill="#e2f4f3">session A: step 2</text>
  <rect x="192" y="112" width="96" height="18" rx="3" fill="#fff" fill-opacity="0.15"/>
  <text x="240" y="125" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"
        fill="#e2f4f3">session B: step 1</text>
  <rect x="192" y="136" width="96" height="18" rx="3" fill="#fff" fill-opacity="0.15"/>
  <text x="240" y="149" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"
        fill="#e2f4f3">session C: step 3</text>
  <text x="240" y="178" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"
        fill="#e2f4f3">If it fails → sessions lost</text>

  <!-- STATELESS (dir) -->
  <rect x="360" y="35" width="300" height="185" rx="8"
        fill="#1ab3a6" fill-opacity="0.06" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="510" y="55" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">Stateless</text>

  <!-- Cliente B -->
  <rect x="375" y="65" width="80" height="30" rx="5" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="415" y="85" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Client B</text>

  <!-- Arrow → stateless server -->
  <line x1="457" y1="80" x2="518" y2="80" stroke="#1ab3a6"
        stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#sf2)"/>
  <text x="488" y="73" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"
        fill="#1ab3a6">complete request</text>

  <!-- Servidor 1 stateless -->
  <rect x="520" y="60" width="56" height="50" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="548" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Srv 1</text>
  <text x="548" y="98" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7"
        fill="#0a3d4a">no session</text>
  <!-- Servidor 2 stateless -->
  <rect x="520" y="120" width="56" height="50" rx="5" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="548" y="140" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Srv 2</text>
  <text x="548" y="158" text-anchor="middle" font-size="7"
        fill="#0a3d4a">no session</text>

  <!-- Arrows: client → both servers -->
  <line x1="457" y1="80" x2="518" y2="85" stroke="#1ab3a6"
        stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
  <line x1="457" y1="80" x2="518" y2="145" stroke="#1ab3a6"
        stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/>

  <text x="510" y="192" text-anchor="middle" font-size="8"
        fill="#0a3d4a">Any server responds — no sync required.</text>

  <text x="340" y="232" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14"
        fill="#64748b">
    Figure 7.1 — Stateful: session on the server; Stateless: each request is autonomous
  </text>

  <defs>
    <marker id="sf1" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6"
            refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#0d7c8c"/></marker>
    <marker id="sf2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6"
            refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

The most accessible illustration of *stateless* is a web search: each
request to a search engine includes the terms, language filters and
location. The server has no knowledge of what was searched before — there
is no session to maintain. The same request can be processed by any
server instance in the data centre. It is precisely this model that
`REST` generalises to any type of resource — including clinical resources.

::: {.callout-note}
**Note** — The term *stateless* refers to session state on the server,
not to the state of data. A `REST` server has state in its data (resources
exist and are persisted); what it lacks is memory of the conversation
with the client. Data is maintained — the session is not.
:::

## `CRUD` and `HTTP` Methods {#sec-rest-crud}

### The four fundamental operations

`CRUD` — Create, Read, Update, Delete — describes the four operations
possible on any information resource. These are primitive and complete
operations: any data manipulation can be expressed as a combination of
the four. In `REST`, each `CRUD` operation has a corresponding `HTTP`
method, which creates a universal vocabulary shared by any implementation
[@RFC9110; @RichardsonRuby2007].

| `CRUD` Operation | `HTTP` Method | Semantics | Idempotent? |
|------------------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| **C**reate | `POST` | Creates a new resource; ID assigned by the server | No |
| **R**ead | `GET` | Reads a resource; does not alter data | Yes |
| **U**pdate | `PUT` | Replaces the resource in its entirety | Yes |
| **U**pdate (partial) | `PATCH` | Updates only the specified fields | Not guaranteed |
| **D**elete | `DELETE` | Removes the resource permanently | Yes |

: CRUD mapping — HTTP methods [@RFC9110]. {#tbl-crud-http}

The property of **idempotency** deserves attention: a method is
idempotent when executing it N times produces the same result as
executing it once. `GET`, `PUT` and `DELETE` are idempotent — repeating
the request has no additional effect. `POST` is not idempotent: sending
the same `POST` twice creates two distinct resources. This distinction
is relevant in clinical environments where network reliability cannot be
guaranteed — a client that does not know whether its request was received
can safely repeat a `GET` or a `DELETE`, but must not repeat a `POST`
without first verifying whether the resource has already been created
[@RFC9110].

### Structure of an `HTTP` request and response

All `HTTP` communication follows a fixed cycle: the client sends a
**request** and the server returns a **response**. Both have a normalised
structure [@RFC9110].

A request is composed of three parts:

1. **Request line** — HTTP method (see table @tbl-crud-http) + `URL` +
   `HTTP` version. Example: `GET /api/courses/42 HTTP/1.1`.
2. **Headers** — metadata: destination server, accepted content type,
   authentication credentials. Plain text, inspectable with any tool
   (curl, Postman, browser). The `Host` header is mandatory; the rest
   are optional.
3. **Body** — data sent (used in `POST` and `PUT`; absent in `GET` and
   `DELETE`).

A response is composed of:

1. **Status line** — `HTTP` version + status code + message. Example:
   `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`
2. **Headers** — response content type, size, location of the created
   resource (in `POST`).
3. **Body** — the requested resource, typically in `JSON`.

```
GET /api/utentes/P-12345 HTTP/1.1
Host: fhir.snssul.min-saude.pt
Accept: application/json
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGci...
```

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 412

{
  "id": "P-12345",
  "nome": "Ana Silva",
  "dataNascimento": "1978-04-22",
  "nrUtente": "123456789"
}
```

### `HTTP` status codes

Status codes standardise the server's response. They are grouped by
family — the first digit identifies the category [@RFC9110]:

| Code | Name | Meaning in a clinical context |
|------|------|-------------------------------|
| `200` | OK | Request successful; resource returned in the body |
| `201` | Created | Resource created; URL of the new resource in the `Location` header |
| `204` | No Content | Request successful; no body (common in `DELETE`) |
| `400` | Bad Request | Malformed request — invalid parameters or incorrect `JSON` |
| `401` | Unauthorized | Authentication required or invalid token |
| `403` | Forbidden | Authenticated but lacking permission to access the resource |
| `404` | Not Found | The requested resource does not exist on the server |
| `409` | Conflict | Version conflict — resource was modified by another client |
| `500` | Internal Server Error | Server-side error, unrelated to the request |

: Most relevant HTTP status codes in a clinical context [@RFC9110]. {#tbl-http-status}

The distinction between `401` and `403` is frequently confused: `401`
means "I do not know who you are"; `403` means "I know who you are, but
you may not access this resource". In clinical systems, a `403` may
indicate that the clinician lacks authorisation to access the patient
record in question — a situation distinct from not having valid
credentials.

### Examples in a hospital context

The following table presents typical clinical operations expressed in
`CRUD`/`HTTP`:

| Clinical operation | Method | URL | Note |
|--------------------|--------|-----|------|
| Retrieve patient data | `GET` | `/Patient/P-12345` | Idempotent — safe to repeat |
| Register new patient | `POST` | `/Patient` | Server assigns the ID |
| Update address | `PUT` | `/Patient/P-12345` | Replaces the entire resource |
| Correct date of birth | `PATCH` | `/Patient/P-12345` | Updates only the field |
| Cancel appointment | `DELETE` | `/Appointment/A-789` | Removes the resource |
| List patient observations | `GET` | `/Observation?patient=P-12345` | Search parameter |

: Clinical operations mapped to CRUD/HTTP (examples with FHIR resource names). {#tbl-crud-clinico}

::: {.callout-note}
**Note** — In clinical systems, `DELETE` rarely physically removes the
record. For audit and traceability reasons, the standard practice is to
mark the resource as inactive or cancelled (*soft delete*), keeping the
history accessible. The operation remains semantically a `DELETE` — the
observable result is that the resource is no longer returned in normal
searches.
:::

## *Endpoints* and URLs — Structure and Search Parameters {#sec-rest-endpoints}

### What an *endpoint* is

An *endpoint* is a specific address that a `REST` server exposes to allow
interaction with a resource type. It is identified by a `URL`
— Uniform Resource Locator — and an `HTTP` method.

The combination of method + `URL` uniquely defines the operation:
`GET /Patient/123` and `POST /Patient` are two different *endpoints*,
even though they share the same base path.

### Anatomy of a `REST` `URL`

A well-formed `REST` `URL` follows a predictable hierarchical structure
[@RFC3986]:

```
https://fhir.snssul.min-saude.pt/api/v1/Patient/P-12345?_format=json
│       │                            │   │        │      │
│       │                            │   │        │      └─ search parameter
│       │                            │   │        └───────── resource id
│       │                            │   └────────────────── resource type
│       │                            └────────────────────── base path (API version)
│       └─────────────────────────────────────────────────── server
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── protocol
```

Each component has a defined role:

- **Protocol** (`https://`) — `HTTPS` ensures the communication is
  encrypted. `HTTP` is acceptable in local development; never in
  production with clinical data.
- **Server** — domain name of the server hosting the `API`. Different
  subdomains may host distinct `APIs`: `fhir.hospital.pt` for clinical
  data, `pacs.hospital.pt` for imaging.
- **Base path** — prefix identifying the `API` and, frequently, its
  version (`/api/v1`). Allows the `API` to evolve without breaking
  existing clients: a client using `/v1` continues to function when
  `/v2` is released.
- **Resource type** — name of the collection, by convention in singular
  or plural. In FHIR: `Patient`, `Observation`, `DiagnosticReport`,
  `Appointment`.
- **Resource ID** — unique identifier of the resource within this
  collection. If omitted (`GET /Patient`), the request returns the
  collection (list of all resources, typically with pagination).
- **Search parameters** — after `?`, filters and modifiers.
  See section 7.6.

### Naming conventions

Naming conventions for `REST` *endpoints* have evolved into a set of
widely adopted practices [@RichardsonRuby2007]:

- Nouns, not verbs — `/Patient` and not `/getPatient` or
  `/createPatient`; the verb is the `HTTP` method
- Hierarchy for relationships — `/Patient/123/Observation` for
  the observations of patient 123
- Opaque IDs — the client must not infer semantics from the ID;
  `/Patient/abc-123` is preferable to `/Patient/2026-silva-ana`
- Version in the base path — `/api/v1/Patient` allows the `API` to
  evolve without breaking clients

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: anatomy of a clinical REST URL.
  Decomposition into annotated boxes.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="180" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- URL como texto -->
  <rect x="20" y="30" width="640" height="36" rx="6" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="40" y="53" font-size="11" font-family="monospace"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">https://</text>
  <text x="115" y="53" font-size="11" font-family="monospace"
        fill="#e2f4f3">fhir.hospital.pt</text>
  <text x="248" y="53" font-size="11" font-family="monospace"
        fill="#1ab3a6">/api/v1/</text>
  <text x="325" y="53" font-size="11" font-family="monospace"
        fill="#fff">Patient</text>
  <text x="390" y="53" font-size="11" font-family="monospace"
        fill="#e2f4f3">/P-12345</text>
  <text x="457" y="53" font-size="11" font-family="monospace"
        fill="#1ab3a6">?_format=json</text>

  <!-- Annotation arrows -->
  <!-- Protocolo -->
  <line x1="68" y1="66" x2="68" y2="90" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <rect x="20" y="91" width="96" height="22" rx="4" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="68" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#fff">Protocol (HTTPS)</text>

  <!-- Servidor -->
  <line x1="195" y1="66" x2="195" y2="90" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <rect x="138" y="91" width="108" height="22" rx="4" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="192" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#fff">Server</text>

  <!-- Base path -->
  <line x1="285" y1="66" x2="285" y2="120" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <rect x="234" y="121" width="100" height="22" rx="4" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="284" y="136" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#fff">Base path + version</text>

  <!-- Tipo de recurso -->
  <line x1="362" y1="66" x2="362" y2="90" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <rect x="308" y="91" width="108" height="22" rx="4" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="362" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#fff">Resource type</text>

  <!-- ID -->
  <line x1="430" y1="66" x2="430" y2="120" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <rect x="378" y="121" width="104" height="22" rx="4" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="430" y="136" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#fff">System ID</text>

  <!-- Parâmetros -->
  <line x1="540" y1="66" x2="540" y2="90" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <rect x="482" y="91" width="130" height="22" rx="4" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="547" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#fff">Search parameters</text>

  <text x="340" y="170" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 7.2 — Anatomy of a clinical REST URL
  </text>
</svg>
</div>
```

### *Endpoints* in a clinical context

To make this concrete, consider a FHIR server at a hospital. The
*endpoints* follow exactly the same generic pattern, with resource names
specific to the FHIR standard:

| Method | URL | Clinical operation |
|--------|-----|--------------------|
| `GET` | `/Patient/P-12345` | Retrieve data for patient with ID P-12345 |
| `GET` | `/Patient?identifier=123456789` | Search for patient by NHS number |
| `POST` | `/Patient` | Register a new patient |
| `GET` | `/Observation?patient=P-12345` | List all clinical observations for the patient |
| `GET` | `/Observation?patient=P-12345&date=2025` | Patient observations in 2025 |
| `GET` | `/DiagnosticReport?patient=P-12345&category=LAB` | Patient laboratory reports |
| `PUT` | `/Appointment/A-789` | Update an appointment |
| `DELETE` | `/Appointment/A-789` | Cancel an appointment |

: Examples of REST endpoints in an FHIR hospital context. {#tbl-endpoints-clinicos}

## `JSON` — Structure, Data Types and Readability {#sec-rest-json}

### What `JSON` is

`JSON` — JavaScript Object Notation — is the most widely used data
serialisation format in web `APIs`. Defined by the `ECMA-404` standard
[@ECMA404], it is plain text structured as key–value pairs, human-readable
and directly processable by any modern programming language.

Despite the name including *JavaScript*, `JSON` is completely independent
of any language. In Python, Java, C#, R or any other language, native
functions exist for serialising and deserialising `JSON` without external
libraries.

### Fundamental syntax

`JSON` syntax is defined by six rules:

1. **Object** — a collection of key–value pairs delimited by curly
   braces `{` `}`. Keys are always `strings` in double quotes. Pairs
   are separated by commas.
2. **Array** — an ordered sequence of values delimited by square brackets
   `[` `]`. Elements are separated by commas.
3. **String** — a sequence of characters in double quotes.
   Example: `"Ana Silva"`.
4. **Number** — an integer or decimal number, without quotes.
   Example: `42`, `3.14`.
5. **Boolean** — `true` or `false`, without quotes.
6. **Null** — an explicit null value: `null`.

Any value may be an object, array, string, number, boolean or null —
and objects and arrays may be nested to any depth.

### Data types in `JSON`

```json
{
  "id": "OBS-001",
  "codigo": "8480-6",
  "designacao": "Tensão arterial sistólica",
  "valor": 122,
  "unidade": "mmHg",
  "dataHora": "2025-11-14T09:30:00+00:00",
  "critico": false,
  "componentes": [
    { "codigo": "8480-6", "valor": 122, "designacao": "Sistólica" },
    { "codigo": "8462-4", "valor": 78,  "designacao": "Diastólica" }
  ],
  "notas": null
}
```

In this example:

- `"id"`, `"codigo"`, `"designacao"`, `"unidade"`, `"dataHora"`
  — strings (in double quotes)
- `"valor"` → `122` — integer number, without quotes
- `"critico"` → `false` — boolean
- `"componentes"` → `[...]` — array of objects (two elements)
- `"notas"` → `null` — explicit null value (the field exists but has
  no value)

::: {.callout-note}
**Note** — In `JSON`, the difference between `"122"` (string) and `122`
(number) is semantic, not merely a matter of format. A system that
expects a number and receives a string may fail or behave incorrectly.
Dates and times are always strings in `JSON` (there is no native `date`
type) — the normalised representation is `ISO 8601`:
`"2025-11-14T09:30:00+00:00"`.
:::

### `JSON` vs `XML` in a clinical context

`XML` — Extensible Markup Language — was the dominant format in the
`APIs` of the previous generation (Web Services `SOAP`, HL7 v3 `CDA`).
A direct comparison illustrates the practical differences:

```xml
<!-- XML — clinical observation -->
<observacao>
  <id>OBS-001</id>
  <codigo>8480-6</codigo>
  <designacao>Tensão arterial sistólica</designacao>
  <valor tipo="numero">122</valor>
  <unidade>mmHg</unidade>
  <critico tipo="booleano">false</critico>
</observacao>
```

```json
{
  "id": "OBS-001",
  "codigo": "8480-6",
  "designacao": "Tensão arterial sistólica",
  "valor": 122,
  "unidade": "mmHg",
  "critico": false
}
```

| Characteristic | `XML` | `JSON` |
|----------------|-------|--------|
| Structure | Opening and closing tags per field | Key–value pair |
| Verbosity | Moderate — repeated tags | Low — compact structure |
| Native data types | No (everything is text; types via attribute or schema) | Yes (string, number, boolean, null, array, object) |
| Arrays | Repeated element without explicit marker | Explicit `[ ]` |
| Support in modern languages | Partial (manual parsing or library) | Universal (`JSON.parse` / equivalent) |
| Current use in healthcare | HL7 v3 CDA, DICOM SR (legacy) | FHIR, modern REST APIs |

: JSON vs XML comparison in the context of clinical APIs [@ECMA404]. {#tbl-json-xml}

### A complete clinical resource in `JSON`

The following example represents a patient conforming to the FHIR model,
in `JSON`, to illustrate how a real clinical resource is structured:

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Patient",
  "id": "P-12345",
  "meta": {
    "versionId": "3",
    "lastUpdated": "2025-11-14T08:00:00+00:00"
  },
  "identifier": [
    {
      "system": "https://www.sns.gov.pt/utente",
      "value": "123456789"
    }
  ],
  "name": [
    {
      "use": "official",
      "family": "Silva",
      "given": ["Ana", "Maria"]
    }
  ],
  "gender": "female",
  "birthDate": "1978-04-22",
  "address": [
    {
      "line": ["Rua do Comércio, 45"],
      "city": "Vila Real",
      "postalCode": "5000-678",
      "country": "PT"
    }
  ],
  "telecom": [
    { "system": "phone", "value": "+351 259 123 456" }
  ]
}
```

Each field has a precise role:

- `"resourceType"` — identifies the FHIR resource type; always present
- `"id"` — the system ID: a technical identifier assigned by the server;
  it is what appears in the URL `/Patient/P-12345`
- `"meta"` — resource metadata: version (`versionId`) and last update
  (`lastUpdated`)
- `"identifier"` — array of clinical identifiers: the NHS patient number;
  may contain several (record number, NIF, etc.)
- `"name"`, `"address"`, `"telecom"` — arrays because a person may have
  multiple names, addresses and contacts

Note the distinction between `"id"` (system ID) and `"identifier"`
(clinical ID). The `"id"` is technical and internal to the server — it
may change if data are migrated to another system. The NHS patient number
in `"identifier"` is the real-world identifier, independent of any
technology, recognised and used by clinicians in day-to-day practice.

## *Search Parameters* and *Chaining* — Composite Searches {#sec-rest-search}

### Basic search parameters

In `REST`, resource searches are performed through **search parameters**
(*search parameters*) appended to the `URL` after the `?` symbol. Each
parameter is a `name=value` pair. Multiple parameters are combined with
`&`, forming a logical AND [@RFC3986; @RichardsonRuby2007].

The general structure is:

```
GET /[ResourceType]?[parameter1]=[value1]&[parameter2]=[value2]
```

Progressive examples with clinical resources:

```
# Single parameter: observations for a patient
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345

# Two parameters (AND): observations for a patient within a period
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345&date=ge2025-01-01

# Three parameters: laboratory observations for a patient in 2025
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345&date=ge2025-01-01&category=laboratory

# Pagination: first 10 results, sorted by date
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345&_count=10&_sort=-date
```

Parameters beginning with `_` (underscore) are control parameters,
common to all resource types:

| Control parameter | Function |
|-------------------|----------|
| `_count` | Maximum number of results per page |
| `_sort` | Sort field (prefix `-` for descending) |
| `_format` | Response format (`json`, `xml`) |
| `_include` | Include referenced resources in the response |
| `_summary` | Return only essential fields (summary) |

: Common REST control parameters (used in FHIR) [@RFC9110]. {#tbl-control-params}

### Search modifiers

Beyond simple equality, search parameters support **modifiers** that
enable more expressive queries. In FHIR, numeric and date comparison
modifiers use two-character prefixes:

| Modifier | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `eq` (omissible) | Equal to | `?valor=120` |
| `ne` | Not equal to | `?status=ne cancelled` |
| `gt` | Greater than | `?date=gt2025-01-01` |
| `lt` | Less than | `?date=lt2025-12-31` |
| `ge` | Greater than or equal | `?date=ge2025-01-01` |
| `le` | Less than or equal | `?date=le2025-12-31` |
| `:contains` | String contains | `?name:contains=silva` |
| `:exact` | Exact match | `?name:exact=Ana Silva` |

: REST/FHIR search modifiers [@RFC3986]. {#tbl-search-modifiers}

Combined examples in a hospital context:

```
# Laboratory reports between two dates
GET /DiagnosticReport?patient=P-12345
  &date=ge2025-01-01
  &date=le2025-12-31
  &category=LAB

# Patients whose family name contains "silva"
GET /Patient?family:contains=silva

# Observations with systolic blood pressure above 140
GET /Observation?code=8480-6&value-quantity=gt140
```

### *Chaining* — chained searches

*Chaining* is an advanced search capability that allows filtering a
resource type based on attributes of a referenced resource — without
requiring multiple sequential `HTTP` requests [@RichardsonRuby2007].

The notation uses a full stop (`.`) as the separator between the
reference and the attribute of the referenced resource:

```
GET /[ResourceType]?[reference-field].[referenced-attribute]=[value]
```

To illustrate the concept, consider a clinical scenario: retrieve all
observations whose patient has a particular NHS number.

**Without *chaining*** (two sequential requests):

```
# Step 1: find the patient ID by NHS number
GET /Patient?identifier=123456789
→ returns { "id": "P-12345", ... }

# Step 2: use the obtained ID to search for observations
GET /Observation?patient=P-12345
```

**With *chaining*** (a single request):

```
GET /Observation?patient.identifier=123456789
```

The server resolves the reference internally: it traverses the
observations, navigates to the patient referenced in each one, checks the
identifier, and returns only the observations whose patient holds that
NHS number.

```{=html}
<!--
  Diagram: chaining — internal reference resolution.
  Teal palette.
-->
<div style="overflow-x:auto; margin:1.5rem 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     style="width:100%;max-width:680px;font-family:sans-serif;">
  <rect width="680" height="200" rx="10" fill="#f8fbfc"/>

  <!-- Título -->
  <text x="340" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#0a3d4a">
    Chaining — GET /Observation?patient.identifier=123456789
  </text>

  <!-- Observation (esq) -->
  <rect x="20" y="40" width="150" height="90" rx="8" fill="#0a3d4a"/>
  <text x="95" y="62" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Observation</text>
  <text x="95" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2f4f3">id: OBS-001</text>
  <text x="95" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2f4f3">code: 8480-6</text>
  <text x="95" y="110" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        fill="#1ab3a6">patient → P-12345</text>

  <!-- Arrow: navigate reference -->
  <line x1="172" y1="95" x2="258" y2="95" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#ch1)"/>
  <text x="215" y="87" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        fill="#0d7c8c">navigate ref.</text>

  <!-- Patient (centro) -->
  <rect x="260" y="40" width="160" height="90" rx="8" fill="#0d7c8c"/>
  <text x="340" y="62" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">Patient</text>
  <text x="340" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#e2f4f3">id: P-12345</text>
  <text x="340" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        fill="#1ab3a6">identifier: 123456789</text>
  <text x="340" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        fill="#e2f4f3">name: Ana Silva</text>

  <!-- Arrow: check criterion -->
  <line x1="422" y1="95" x2="500" y2="95" stroke="#1ab3a6" stroke-width="2"
        marker-end="url(#ch2)"/>
  <text x="461" y="87" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9"
        fill="#1ab3a6">check criterion</text>

  <!-- Resultado (dir) -->
  <rect x="502" y="50" width="158" height="70" rx="8" fill="#1ab3a6"/>
  <text x="581" y="72" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10"
        font-weight="bold" fill="#fff">OBS-001 included</text>
  <text x="581" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">identifier = 123456789</text>
  <text x="581" y="107" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">condition satisfied</text>

  <!-- Notes at the bottom -->
  <rect x="20" y="144" width="640" height="30" rx="5"
        fill="#e2f4f3" stroke="#0d7c8c" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="340" y="164" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#0a3d4a">
    Without chaining: 2 sequential HTTP requests.
    With chaining: 1 request — the server resolves the reference internally.
  </text>

  <text x="340" y="192" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#64748b">
    Figure 7.3 — Chaining: reference navigation in a single query
  </text>

  <defs>
    <marker id="ch1" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6"
            refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#0d7c8c"/></marker>
    <marker id="ch2" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6"
            refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1ab3a6"/></marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
</div>
```

### *Chaining* examples in a hospital context

```
# Observations for female patients
GET /Observation?patient.gender=female

# DiagnosticReports for patients born after 1990
GET /DiagnosticReport?patient.birthdate=gt1990-01-01

# Observations performed by a clinician from a specific department
GET /Observation?performer.organization.name=Laboratório+Central

# Double chaining: observations for patients with address in Vila Real
GET /Observation?patient.address-city=Vila+Real
```

*Chaining* can be nested across multiple levels (separated by additional
full stops), although the supported depth varies across implementations.
The principle remains: each full stop traverses a reference between
resources.

::: {.callout-note}
**Note** — *Chaining* significantly increases search expressiveness, but
carries a performance cost: the server must resolve references internally,
which may entail multiple database operations. In production FHIR servers,
deep *chaining* queries are frequently subject to timeout or complexity
limits. When implementing clients, simple *chaining* (one level) should
be preferred wherever possible.
:::

## Proposed Exercises {#sec-rest-exercicios .unnumbered}

**1.** Consider the following statements about `REST`:
(a) "`REST` is a communication protocol";
(b) "A `RESTful` system must use `JSON`";
(c) "The *stateless* constraint means that the server does not store
persistent data".
Identify which statements are false and justify your answer.

**2.** A server returns status code `403` in response to
`GET /Patient/P-12345`. Another server returns `404` for the same
request. What is the semantic difference between the two responses?
What implication does each have for the client?

**3.** A telemedicine system needs to retrieve, for a patient identified
by their NHS number, all clinical observations recorded in the past year.
Write the sequence of `HTTP` requests required:
(a) without using *chaining* (two requests);
(b) with *chaining* (a single request).

**4.** Analyse the following `JSON` fragment and identify:
(a) the number of top-level key–value pairs;
(b) the fields with an `array` value;
(c) the field with a `boolean` value;
(d) the field with a `null` value.

```json
{
  "resourceType": "Observation",
  "id": "OBS-789",
  "status": "final",
  "code": {
    "coding": [
      { "system": "http://loinc.org", "code": "2345-7" }
    ],
    "text": "Glicemia em jejum"
  },
  "valueQuantity": { "value": 5.6, "unit": "mmol/L" },
  "interpretation": null,
  "issued": "2025-11-14T10:00:00+00:00",
  "performer": ["Laboratório Central"],
  "note": []
}
```

**5.** The *endpoint* `GET /Patient?family:contains=ferreira&address-city=Braga`
returns a list of patients. Explain in plain language the search criterion
expressed by this request. Then reformulate the request to also return
only male patients, sorted by date of birth in descending order, with
pagination of 20 results per page.

**6.** A hospital wishes to export to a statistical analysis system all
systolic blood pressure observations (LOINC code `8480-6`) recorded
between 1 January and 31 March 2025 for patients aged over 65. Describe
the `REST` search strategy for obtaining these data, identifying the
search parameters and any use of *chaining*.
